source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure VM Disks for Critical VMs Are Encrypted With Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK)

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure VM Disks for Critical VMs Are Encrypted With Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK)
input_row.description: Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) are a feature in Google Cloud Storage and Google Compute Engine. If you supply your own encryption keys, Google uses your key to protect the Google-generated keys used to encrypt and decrypt your data. By default, Google Compute Engine encrypts all data at rest. Compute Engine handles and manages this encryption for you without any additional actions on your part. However, if you wanted to control and manage this encryption yourself, you can provide your own encryption keys.
input_row.rationale: By default, Google Compute Engine encrypts all data at rest. Compute Engine handles and manages this encryption for you without any additional actions on your part. However, if you wanted to control and manage this encryption yourself, you can provide your own encryption keys. If you provide your own encryption keys, Compute Engine uses your key to protect the Google-generated keys used to encrypt and decrypt your data. Only users who can provide the correct key can use resources protected by a customer-supplied encryption key. Google does not store your keys on its servers and cannot access your protected data unless you provide the key. This also means that if you forget or lose your key, there is no way for Google to recover the key or to recover any data encrypted with the lost key. At least business critical VMs should have VM disks encrypted with CSEK. Impact: If you lose your encryption key, you will not be able to recover the data.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to Compute Engine Disks by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/disks. 2. Click on the disk for your critical VMs to see its configuration details. 3. Ensure that Encryption type is set to Customer supplied. From Google Cloud CLI Ensure diskEncryptionKey property in the below command's response is not null, and contains key sha256 with corresponding value gcloud compute disks describe <DISK_NAME> --zone <ZONE> -- format="json(diskEncryptionKey,name)"
input_row.remediation: Currently there is no way to update the encryption of an existing disk. Therefore you should create a new disk with Encryption set to Customer supplied. From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to Compute Engine Disks by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/disks. 2. Click CREATE DISK. 3. Set Encryption type to Customer supplied, 4. Provide the Key in the box. 5. Select Wrapped key. 6. Click Create. From Google Cloud CLI In the gcloud compute tool, encrypt a disk using the --csek-key-file flag during instance creation. If you are using an RSA-wrapped key, use the gcloud beta component: gcloud compute instances create <INSTANCE_NAME> --csek-key-file <example- file.json> To encrypt a standalone persistent disk: gcloud compute disks create <DISK_NAME> --csek-key-file <example-file.json> Default Value: By default, VM disks are encrypted with Google-managed keys. They are not encrypted with Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/customer-supplied- encryption#encrypt_a_new_persistent_disk_with_your_own_keys 2. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/reference/rest/v1/disks/get 3. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/customer-supplied- encryption#key_file Additional Information: Note 1: When you delete a persistent disk, Google discards the cipher keys, rendering the data irretrievable. This process is irreversible. Note 2: It is up to you to generate and manage your key. You must provide a key that is a 256-bit string encoded in RFC 4648 standard base64 to Compute Engine. Note 3: An example key file looks like this. [ { "uri": "https://www.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/myproject/zones/us- central1-a/disks/example-disk", "key": "acXTX3rxrKAFTF0tYVLvydU1riRZTvUNC4g5I11NY-c=", "key-type": "raw" }, { "uri": "https://www.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/myproject/global/snapshots/my -private-snapshot", "key": "ieCx/NcW06PcT7Ep1X6LUTc/hLvUDYyzSZPPVCVPTVEohpeHASqC8uw5TzyO9U+Fka9JFHz0mBib XUInrC/jEk014kCK/NPjYgEMOyssZ4ZINPKxlUh2zn1bV+MCaTICrdmuSBTWlUUiFoDD6PYznLwh8 ZNdaheCeZ8ewEXgFQ8V+sDroLaN3Xs3MDTXQEMMoNUXMCZEIpg9Vtp9x2oeQ5lAbtt7bYAAHf5l+g JWw3sUfs0/Glw5fpdjT8Uggrr+RMZezGrltJEF293rvTIjWOEB3z5OHyHwQkvdrPDFcTqsLfh+8Hr 8g+mf+7zVPEC8nEbqpdl3GPv3A7AwpFp7MA==" "key-type": "rsa-encrypted" } ]
input_row.impact: If you lose your encryption key, you will not be able to recover the data.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/customer-supplied- encryption#encrypt_a_new_persistent_disk_with_your_own_keys 2. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/reference/rest/v1/disks/get 3. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/customer-supplied- encryption#key_file Additional Information: Note 1: When you delete a persistent disk, Google discards the cipher keys, rendering the data irretrievable. This process is irreversible. Note 2: It is up to you to generate and manage your key. You must provide a key that is a 256-bit string encoded in RFC 4648 standard base64 to Compute Engine. Note 3: An example key file looks like this. [ { "uri": "https://www.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/myproject/zones/us- central1-a/disks/example-disk", "key": "acXTX3rxrKAFTF0tYVLvydU1riRZTvUNC4g5I11NY-c=", "key-type": "raw" }, { "uri": "https://www.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/myproject/global/snapshots/my -private-snapshot", "key": "ieCx/NcW06PcT7Ep1X6LUTc/hLvUDYyzSZPPVCVPTVEohpeHASqC8uw5TzyO9U+Fka9JFHz0mBib XUInrC/jEk014kCK/NPjYgEMOyssZ4ZINPKxlUh2zn1bV+MCaTICrdmuSBTWlUUiFoDD6PYznLwh8 ZNdaheCeZ8ewEXgFQ8V+sDroLaN3Xs3MDTXQEMMoNUXMCZEIpg9Vtp9x2oeQ5lAbtt7bYAAHf5l+g JWw3sUfs0/Glw5fpdjT8Uggrr+RMZezGrltJEF293rvTIjWOEB3z5OHyHwQkvdrPDFcTqsLfh+8Hr 8g+mf+7zVPEC8nEbqpdl3GPv3A7AwpFp7MA==" "key-type": "rsa-encrypted" } ]

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control can be fully verified via GCP APIs/Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/GCP CLI.
- The audit mentions the use of the `gcloud compute disks describe` command, which confirms the existence of a method to programmatically verify the encryption status of VM disks.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_compute_disk_csek_encryption_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Utilize the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) to check if critical VM disks are encrypted with Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK).
- Use the command:
  ```
  gcloud compute disks describe <DISK_NAME> --zone <ZONE> --format="json(diskEncryptionKey[name, sha256])"
  ```
- Enumerate all disks associated with critical VMs within the relevant project and zones.
- For each disk, check the existence of the `diskEncryptionKey` property and confirm that it contains a non-null `sha256` value, indicative of CSEK usage.
- Pass Criteria: The `diskEncryptionKey` has a `sha256` value, denoting the disk is encrypted using a Customer-Supplied Encryption Key.
- Fail Criteria: The `diskEncryptionKey` is null or absent, indicating the disk is not encrypted with CSEK.
- Document findings with details on which disks are compliant or non-compliant with the CSEK encryption requirement.
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **PARTIALLY_AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is **mostly correct**. The encryption status of disks (including CSEK usage) can be programmatically checked via GCP APIs and the `gcloud` CLI.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI Capability:**  
  The `gcloud compute disks describe` command can retrieve the `diskEncryptionKey` property, which includes CSEK information. The GCP REST API (`compute.disks.get`) also exposes this.
- **Caveat:**  
  The assessment assumes that "critical VMs" are already identified. The process of identifying which VMs are "critical" is typically **manual** or policy-driven and not natively automatable via GCP APIs. Only after this identification can the disk check be automated.
- **Conclusion:**  
  The encryption check is automatable, but the end-to-end control is only fully automatable if the list of "critical VMs" is provided as input.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **gcloud Command:**  
  The command  
  ```
  gcloud compute disks describe <DISK_NAME> --zone <ZONE> --format="json(diskEncryptionKey[name, sha256])"
  ```
  is correct and will return the relevant encryption key information.
- **Automation Logic:**  
  The logic to check for a non-null `diskEncryptionKey.sha256` is **mostly correct** for detecting CSEK. However, the presence of `sha256` alone does not guarantee CSEK; it could also indicate CMEK (Customer-Managed Encryption Key) if not carefully parsed.  
  - For CSEK, the `diskEncryptionKey` object will have a `sha256` field, but so will CMEK disks (with a different structure). The field `kmsKeyName` is used for CMEK, while CSEK uses `rawKey` or `sha256`.
- **Program Name:**  
  The program name `gcp_compute_disk_csek_encryption_enabled` is **descriptive and clear**, but could be more specific (see Improvements).
- **Missing Details:**  
  - The automation does not specify how to enumerate disks attached to "critical VMs" (should use `gcloud compute instances list` and `gcloud compute instances describe` to map disks to VMs).
  - No mention of handling regional disks or disks attached to multiple VMs.
  - No explicit handling of CMEK vs CSEK detection.

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Critical VM Identification:**  
  Clarify that the automation requires a pre-identified list of "critical VMs" as input, or specify how "critical" is determined (e.g., via labels, naming conventions, or external inventory).
- **CSEK vs CMEK Distinction:**  
  The automation should explicitly check that the disk is using CSEK, not CMEK.  
  - CSEK: `diskEncryptionKey.sha256` present, and **no** `kmsKeyName` property.
  - CMEK: `diskEncryptionKey.kmsKeyName` present.
  - Add logic to ensure only CSEK disks are marked compliant.
- **Enumerating Disks:**  
  Add steps to enumerate all disks attached to the identified "critical VMs" (using `gcloud compute instances describe <INSTANCE>` and parsing the `disks[].source` field).
- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  To be more precise and follow best practice, consider:  
  `gcp_compute_disk_encryption_csek_at_rest_enabled`
- **Alternative API:**  
  The REST API (`compute.disks.get`) can be used for automation at scale, especially for organizations using custom automation frameworks.
- **Documentation:**  
  Recommend documenting the process for mapping VMs to disks and how "critical" is defined.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **MEDIUM**

- **Reason:**  
  The technical approach is sound for the encryption check, but the lack of clarity on "critical VM" identification and the incomplete distinction between CSEK and CMEK lowers confidence in the assessment's completeness.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Required Modifications:**
  - Clarify that the automation requires a pre-identified list of "critical VMs" or specify the method for identification.
  - Update the logic to distinguish CSEK from CMEK disks.
  - Expand the automation steps to include mapping VMs to their attached disks.
  - Consider updating the program name for clarity and convention.
- **Classification:**  
  Keep as "Automated" **only for the disk encryption check**. The overall control is "Automated" if the "critical VMs" list is provided; otherwise, it is "Partially Automated".

---

**Summary Table**

| Section              | Assessment           | Comments                                                                                  |
|----------------------|---------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation  | PARTIALLY_AGREE     | Disk check is automatable; "critical VM" identification is not natively automatable.      |
| Technical Accuracy   | 8/10                | Command is correct; CSEK vs CMEK distinction and disk enumeration need improvement.        |
| Improvements         | See above           | Add CSEK/CMEK logic, clarify "critical VM" input, improve program name.                   |
| Confidence           | MEDIUM              | Gaps in "critical VM" identification and CSEK/CMEK logic.                                 |
| Final Recommendation | Modify              | Update logic, clarify scope, improve documentation, refine program name.                  |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
The encryption status of Compute Engine disks, including whether Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) are used, is fully exposed via the GCP API and can be programmatically checked using the `gcloud compute disks describe` command or the Compute Engine API. While identifying "critical VMs" may require a manual policy decision, the compliance control and audit step specifically focus on verifying the encryption status of disks already associated with those VMs. Therefore, the technical compliance check itself is fully automatable once the relevant disks are identified.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_compute_disk_csek_encryption_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP Resource/Service:** Compute Engine Disks
- **API/CLI Method:**  
  - Google Cloud SDK (gcloud):  
    `gcloud compute disks describe <DISK_NAME> --zone <ZONE> --format="json(diskEncryptionKey)"`
  - REST API:  
    `compute.disks.get`
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - `diskEncryptionKey` property in the disk resource.
  - Specifically, check for the presence of a non-null `diskEncryptionKey.sha256` and confirm that the `diskEncryptionKey` includes a `rawKey` or `rsaEncryptedKey` (indicative of CSEK, not CMEK).
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - The disk resource has a `diskEncryptionKey` property with a non-null `sha256` value, and the key type matches a Customer-Supplied Encryption Key (CSEK), not a Customer-Managed Encryption Key (CMEK).
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - The `diskEncryptionKey` property is absent, null, or does not correspond to a CSEK (e.g., it references a KMS key instead, which would be CMEK).

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agree that the encryption check is automatable, but the second opinion notes that identifying "critical VMs" is a manual/policy step. However, the compliance control's audit step is strictly about verifying encryption status on already-identified disks, which is fully automatable. Thus, the final decision is "Automated" for the technical compliance check.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
